words.punjabi_note: In Delhi speech छत is used for both roof and ceiling — context makes the meaning clear. The same word with the same pronunciation serves both senses.

true_relatives.note: The same Sanskrit root that gave the roof sense of छत also gives the ceiling sense — a ceiling is the inner canopy of a room
Identical usage in Punjabi — the same word covers both roof and ceiling

sound_alikes.note: Sounds like English 'chat' — imagine looking up at the ceiling while having a chat indoors

root_breakdown.logic: The same word that describes the outer roof also describes the inner ceiling — both are the overhead covering of a space, just viewed from different sides.

etymology_story: Sanskrit छत्र (chatra) described any overhead covering — from a royal canopy to the roof of a building.
The same form passed through Prakrit and settled in Hindi as छत, covering the concept from both sides: the outer roof and the inner ceiling.
In modern Hindi, छत as 'ceiling' specifically refers to the visible underside of the roof inside a room — the surface overhead when you look up from inside.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable; the 'chh' is a heavily aspirated 'ch' — distinctly more breath than English 'ch'

tips.tip: pronounce English 'chat' but with much more breath on the 'ch' — the extra aspiration marks it as distinctly Hindi

memory_hook.primary: A person lying on their back on a चटाई (mat) on the ज़मीन (floor), staring up at a cracked white ceiling with a single fan turning overhead
memory_hook.connection: The ceiling is what you see when you look up from the floor inside a room — छत is the word for that overhead surface, just as it is for the roof above it

examples.hindi: कमरे की छत बहुत ऊँची है।
examples.romanisation: kamre kī chhat bahut ūñcī hai.
examples.english: The ceiling of the room is very high.
examples.literal: room of ceiling very high is

examples.hindi: छत से पानी टपक रहा था।
examples.romanisation: chhat se pānī ṭapak rahā thā.
examples.english: Water was dripping from the ceiling.
examples.literal: ceiling from water dripping was

examples.hindi: बिजली का पंखा छत पर लगा है।
examples.romanisation: bijlī kā pañkhā chhat par lagā hai.
examples.english: The electric fan is fixed to the ceiling.
examples.literal: electricity of fan ceiling on attached is

examples.hindi: उन्होंने छत को सफेद रंग से रंगा।
examples.romanisation: unhõne chhat ko safed raṅg se raṅgā.
examples.english: They painted the ceiling white.
examples.literal: they-by ceiling to white colour with painted

forms.label: compound — electric
forms.hindi: बिजली का / बिजली की / बिजली के
forms.roman: bijlī kā / bijlī kī / bijlī ke

true_relatives.note: बिजली is the vernacular descendant of Sanskrit विद्युत् — the same word was used for both lightning and, later, electricity

useful_coincidences.note: Hebrew ברק (barak) means lightning — the same natural phenomenon that originally gave बिजली its name, making the meaning memorable across languages

sound_alikes.note: बिजली starts with 'bij' which sounds close to 'big' — electricity is BIG power; the 'lee' ending completes the word

root_breakdown.logic: Lightning was the original 'electricity' humans knew — and when electrical technology arrived in India, the same word was extended from natural lightning to man-made electric power.

etymology_story: Sanskrit विद्युत् (vidyut) described the flash of lightning — the most dramatic electrical phenomenon in nature.
Through Prakrit the word transformed gradually into bijulī, keeping the connection to the bright flash.
When electricity was introduced to India, Hindi naturally used बिजली for it — the word already meant 'the brilliant flash from the sky', and man-made electric light was seen as a similar phenomenon.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable 'bij'; the final 'lī' is a long 'ee'

tips.tip: the first syllable 'bij' sounds like English 'big' — a useful anchor for the start of the word

memory_hook.primary: A dark room suddenly flooded with light as a single bulb flickers on after a power cut — the bare bulb glowing against the ceiling
memory_hook.connection: The original meaning of बिजली was lightning — a sudden flash of light from the sky; when electric bulbs arrived, they too were called बिजली because they brought the same kind of sudden brightness

examples.hindi: बिजली चली गई है।
examples.romanisation: bijlī calī gaī hai.
examples.english: The electricity has gone out.
examples.literal: electricity went gone is

examples.hindi: बिजली का बिल बहुत आया है।
examples.romanisation: bijlī kā bil bahut āyā hai.
examples.english: A very high electricity bill has come.
examples.literal: electricity of bill very came is

examples.hindi: रात को बिजली की रोशनी में पढ़ता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: rāt ko bijlī kī rośnī mẽ paṛhtā hū̃.
examples.english: I study in electric light at night.
examples.literal: night to electricity of light in reading am

examples.hindi: तूफान में बिजली गिरी।
examples.romanisation: tūfān mẽ bijlī girī.
examples.english: Lightning struck during the storm.
examples.literal: storm in lightning fell

true_relatives.meaning: that which is to be drunk, drinkable
true_relatives.note: पानी is the vernacular form of Sanskrit पानीय — something fit to be drunk; it comes from the Sanskrit root पा (pā), to drink
The root verb — पानी is literally 'that which you drink'

useful_coincidences.meaning: a flat container, often used for liquids
useful_coincidences.note: A pan holds water — a loose but vivid link from the sound 'pān' to the idea of containing and using water
Russian пить (pit') and Sanskrit पा (pā) are cousins — both mean 'to drink' and both are ancestors of words for water or drink across Indo-European languages

sound_alikes.note: 'paneer' starts with 'pān' — paneer is made with water, and both words share the same opening sound from the same root language family

evolution.meaning: that which is to be drunk

root_breakdown.logic: Water is defined in this word by what you do with it — you drink it. The Sanskrit gerundive pānīya means 'that which is drinkable', and Hindi condensed this into the simple noun पानी.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit root पा (pā) meant 'to drink' — one of the most ancient and basic human actions.
From it came पानीय (pānīya), a gerundive meaning 'that which is fit to be drunk'.
Through Prakrit the form simplified into पानी — the universal Hindi word for water, used for drinking water, cooking water, and rainwater alike.

pronunciation_guide.stress: both syllables are long; the 'ā' in 'pā' and the 'ī' in 'nī' are both sustained vowels

tips.tip: पानी sounds very close to English 'pony' — same rhythm, same two long vowels

memory_hook.primary: A terracotta pot (matka) with water condensing on its outside in the heat, standing on a shelf in a cool Indian kitchen
memory_hook.connection: The matka of water is one of the most iconic images in an Indian घर — पानी is the lifeblood of every kitchen (रसोई) and home

examples.hindi: मुझे पानी चाहिए।
examples.romanisation: mujhe pānī cāhie.
examples.english: I need water.
examples.literal: me-to water needed

examples.hindi: नल से पानी नहीं आ रहा।
examples.romanisation: nal se pānī nahī̃ ā rahā.
examples.english: Water is not coming from the tap.
examples.literal: tap from water not coming

examples.hindi: बाथरूम में गरम पानी की ज़रूरत है।
examples.romanisation: bāthrūm mẽ garam pānī kī zarūrat hai.
examples.english: Hot water is needed in the bathroom.
examples.literal: bathroom in hot water of need is

examples.hindi: बारिश का पानी ज़मीन पर जमा हो गया।
examples.romanisation: bāriś kā pānī zamīn par jamā ho gayā.
examples.english: Rainwater has collected on the ground.
examples.literal: rain of water ground on collected become went

true_relatives.meaning: a substance in gaseous state; cooking gas
true_relatives.note: Direct borrowing from English — the Hindi गैस is the phonetic Indianisation of English 'gas'
The English word 'gas' itself was coined by the Flemish chemist J.B. van Helmont in the 17th century, modelled on Greek χάος (chaos)

sound_alikes.note: गैस is simply the English word 'gas' said with an Indian accent — the 'ai' diphthong replaces the English 'a'

root_breakdown.logic: The 17th-century scientist van Helmont created the word 'gas' from Greek chaos because gas — unlike solid or liquid — has no fixed shape; in Hindi it was borrowed to name the cooking gas supplied in cylinders.

etymology_story: The word 'gas' was invented in the 17th century by the Flemish chemist Jan Baptist van Helmont, who based it on Greek χάος (khaos) — the formless void — because gas has no fixed shape.
Through Dutch and then English it became the standard scientific and domestic term for gaseous substances.
Hindi borrowed it directly as गैस, used almost exclusively in the domestic context of the cooking gas cylinder that is central to every Indian kitchen.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable; the 'ai' is a diphthong — starts like 'a' in 'father', glides toward 'ee'

tips.tip: गैस sounds almost like English 'guys' — the 'ai' diphthong is similar

memory_hook.primary: A red LPG cylinder standing upright in a corner of a small Indian रसोई, a rubber pipe connecting it to the stove burner
memory_hook.connection: The red गैस cylinder is one of the most iconic objects in an Indian kitchen — the word is simply English 'gas' dressed in Hindi clothes

examples.hindi: रसोई में गैस खत्म हो गई।
examples.romanisation: rasoī mẽ gais khatam ho gaī.
examples.english: The gas has run out in the kitchen.
examples.literal: kitchen in gas finished become went

examples.hindi: गैस सिलेंडर बदलवाना होगा।
examples.romanisation: gais silenḍar badlavānā hogā.
examples.english: The gas cylinder will need to be changed.
examples.literal: gas cylinder changed-cause will-be

examples.hindi: गैस का चूल्हा बंद कर दो।
examples.romanisation: gais kā cūlhā band kar do.
examples.english: Turn off the gas stove.
examples.literal: gas of stove closed do give

examples.hindi: उनके घर में पाइप से गैस आती है।
examples.romanisation: unke ghar mẽ pāip se gais ātī hai.
examples.english: Gas comes to their house through a pipe.
examples.literal: their house in pipe from gas comes is

true_relatives.note: Hindi नल is directly inherited from Sanskrit नल (nala), which described a hollow reed or tube — a natural tube through which liquid flows

useful_coincidences.meaning: a projecting spout through which water is directed
useful_coincidences.note: A nozzle and a tap (नल) are the same concept — a tube that controls the flow of water; the 'n' and the function both overlap

sound_alikes.note: नल sounds like English 'null' — when you turn off the tap, water flow becomes null (zero)

evolution.meaning: tap, faucet; also a water pipe

root_breakdown.logic: A hollow reed was the first pipe known to people — water would flow through it naturally. The word transferred to man-made pipes and eventually to taps, which control the flow of water through pipes.

etymology_story: Sanskrit नल (nala) described a hollow reed — the simplest natural tube through which water or air can flow.
This image of a hollow tube passed into Prakrit and then Hindi, where नल came to mean any pipe or conduit.
In modern Hindi, नल is the everyday word for a tap or faucet — the control point at the end of a pipe where water flows out.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — short, clean, no emphasis needed

tips.tip: नल sounds exactly like English 'null' — the same short 'u' vowel and final 'l'

memory_hook.primary: A brass tap over a stone sink in an old Indian courtyard house, a thin stream of water flowing steadily from the spout
memory_hook.connection: The image of the hollow tube (Sanskrit nala = reed) pouring water is built into the word — नल is that hollow tube in its modern form as a tap

examples.hindi: नल बंद कर दो, पानी बर्बाद हो रहा है।
examples.romanisation: nal band kar do, pānī barbād ho rahā hai.
examples.english: Turn off the tap, water is being wasted.
examples.literal: tap closed do give, water wasted becoming is

examples.hindi: रसोई का नल टपक रहा है।
examples.romanisation: rasoī kā nal ṭapak rahā hai.
examples.english: The kitchen tap is dripping.
examples.literal: kitchen of tap dripping is

examples.hindi: ठंडे पानी का नल कौन-सा है?
examples.romanisation: ṭhanḍe pānī kā nal kaun-sā hai?
examples.english: Which is the cold water tap?
examples.literal: cold water of tap which is

examples.hindi: नल खोलते ही पानी आने लगा।
examples.romanisation: nal kholte hī pānī āne lagā.
examples.english: As soon as the tap was opened, water started flowing.
examples.literal: tap opening as-soon water coming began
